t: ACOG Committee Opinion 757 (2018) — perinatal depression screening.
USPSTF — screening in pregnant and postpartum women.
EPDS (Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale, 10 items) — gold standard screening; cut-off ≥ 10 (moderate-severe), ≥ 13 (high clinical probability).
Clinical interview (bedtime or suicide risk assessment).
Postnatal psychosis differential — urgent hospitalization if psychotic symptoms present.
≤ 2 weeks, mild, does not require clinical intervention.
Ego-dystonic intrusions of harm to infant; comorbid postnatal depression.
C-SSRS — suicide and risk of harm to infant.
Psychotherapy is first-line — CBT, IPT (particularly adapted to peripartum context).
Sertraline (least excreted into milk) and paroxetine — first-line;
Fluoxetine — long half-life, use with caution;
36. — IV 60-hour infusion; FDA 2019 approval; monitored in clinic under REMS program; rapid effect.
— oral 14 days; FDA 2023 approval; more convenient.
— psychotic depression, severe suicide risk, impaired food intake, also safe during pregnancy.
Family support, peer support (e.g., PND helpline).
Social service and parent-child bonding support.
Sleep hygiene and involvement of family members in night care.
NICE CG192 — sertraline and paroxetine first-line in breastfeeding.
Wisner K.L. et al. Am J Psychiatry 2013 — SSRI in pregnancy cardiac malformations (paroxetine cautiously).
Meltzer-Brody S. et al. Lancet 2018 — brexanolone RCT.
Perinatal Interpersonal Therapy (IPT) — Stuart (Stuart S.), Robertson (Robertson M.)
Major role transitions, partner relationships, social support system.
26. — Safe in pregnancy and lactation; in severe refractory cases.
Risk of recurrence in subsequent pregnancies (~25%).
Myth 1: "PPD is just ordinary 'baby blues', transient"
baby blues ≤ 2 weeks, mild; PPD clinical depressive episode, requires intervention; can be prolonged.
sertraline and paroxetine are acceptable during breastfeeding; milk transfer minimal; clinical counseling.
Myth 3: "Mother is weak, she should 'fix herself'"
PPD is a clinical condition; risk of suicide and infanticide exists; intervention is required.
Rapid effect, but cost and need for clinic monitoring significant; standard approach SSRI + psychotherapy.
Myth 5: "PPD does not affect the infant"
PPD affects mother-infant bonding and impacts the child's cognitive and emotional development; early intervention is crucial.
Myth 6: "Intrusive thoughts (harming a baby) are always a sign of psychosis"
In postpartum OCD, intrusive thoughts are EGO-DYSTONIC (the patient suffers, avoidance behavior); in postpartum psychosis they are ego-syntonic and can lead to behavior — different intervention.
WHO. ICD-11. 6E20 Mental and behavioural disorders associated with pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium. 2024.
